text:
Village Institutes are part of the wave of educational innovation in the early years of the Turkish Republic. What were originally conceived as schools to train rural teachers in 1940, were adapted for health training for graduates in 1943 in order to deal with the acute lack of doctors and nurses in rural Turkey. Graduates of the Health Branch were trained as village health officers to work in conjunction with the village teachers who had been trained in the Village Institutes since 1940.

Medical Assistance in Colonial Malawi, 1931–1951: Careers and Contributions of Village Health Officers From the pages of colonial records to personal testimonies of the last surviving graduate of the class of 1951, this paper endeavours to relate the careers and contributions of Village Health Officers who were deployed in colonial Malawi. The study draws from both literature and oral history. In this paper oral history interviews carried out with the four surviving graduates of the class of 1951 have been relied upon in addition to colonial records to relate the contributions of the Village Health Officers in colonial Malawi. In the course of the interviews the last of the VHOs who graduated in 1951 and survived until 2014 shared with the researchers his personal experiences as a VHO and gave them documents that he had kept since he was a student VHO. These include his student identification card, medical books appropriate to the time he was a student VHO, his VHO certificate, a certificate for a course he attended at Sahra Sanitary Service School and his permit to practice anaesthesia.

This research shows that the village health officers underwent a more academically demanding course – one suited to the standards of rural Anatolia – and that they were instructed in science and able to apply their training to matters of public health, including the management of contagious illnesses, provision of sewerage and primary care services and the performance of basic surgical operations. Bringing these officials to the limelight bears witness to a crucial phase in the history of Turkish health policies and pedagogy as well as of the new concept of social services that sought to modernize and transform rural living spaces and societies.
headline: Fully Human Written
prediction: We believe that this document is fully human-written
